With О·= min { 1 4Lm,Оұ , вҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұвҲҡ TПғm,Оұ } and C = Gm := Gmmax, Algorithm 2 satisп¬Ғes E[fm(ВҜzT) вҲ’fm(zвҲ—)] вүӨ4Lm,ОұвҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘ2 Оұ T + 3Пғm,ОұвҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұвҲҡ T ,Personalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning with ВҜzT = 1 T вҲ‘TвҲ’1 t=0 zt. Taking ПғО¶ as in (12), we have: E[fm(ВҜzT) вҲ’fm(zвҲ—)] вүІ Lm,ОұвҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘ2 Оұ T + (14) + вҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұ вҲҡ ОұВҜПғ2w,m+ОұЛңПғ2w,m + ВҜПғ2 Оё,m qMT + ОұdwG2mlog(1 Оҙ) M2Пө2 Note that the privacy cost now scales with Gm/M = Gmmax/M instead of G/N in Theorem 4.5. Note that we have Gmmax/M вүҘG/N, with equality if and only if all the mi are equal. This highlights the additional cost of privacy with heterogeneous sample sizes, particularly when some users have much more data than others. Compared to Theorem 4.5, there is also an additional variance term ЛңПғw,m induced by the randomness of client sampling. Note that ЛңПғw,m vanishes when (i) q = 1 (i.e., no sampling), or (ii) вҲҮwfi(wвҲ—,ОёвҲ— i) = 0 for all i, which always holds for the additive model (3). When all users are sampled ( q = 1) and mi = 1 for each user, we have M = N and ЛңПғw,m = 0, and we recover the same bound as in Theorem 4.5, whereT = ncorresponds to the number of samples per user. In this case the bias term de- creases slowly as 1/n. In contrast, if mi = 1 and q= 1/N, i.e., we sample one user per round on average, thenqM = 1, and T now corresponds to the total number of samples Nn. The bias term now decreases as 1/Nn, while the variance decreases at the same 1/ вҲҡ Nn rate. Of course, this comes at the cost of more frequent communication rounds, since each round only uses the data of a single user. Optimizing average user performance. In some cases, we may have clients with heterogeneous amounts of data, but still want to optimize the average performance f in (1), rather than the weighted average fm in (11). This can be achieved by replacing gt Оё,i and gt w,i by averages over the minibatch instead of sums in Algorithm 2. This leads to the following guarantee on the excess risk (see Appendix B.6): E[f(ВҜzT) вҲ’f(zвҲ—)] вүІ (15) LОұвҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘ2 Оұ T + вҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұ вҲҡ ОұВҜПғ2w + ВҜПғ2 Оё qNmminT + ОұdwG2 log(1 Оҙ) N2Пө2 . (16) Note that the privacy cost is similar to the case with homogeneous data in Section 4, rather than the larger cost of Theorem 5.2, since we are now treating each user equally. Nevertheless, the variance term displays an effective total sample size of ntot = qNmminT, which is smaller than in Theorem 5.2, where ntot = qMT corresponds to the expected total number of samples, unless all the mi are equal. This highlights that this improvement in privacy comes at a cost in statistical efп¬Ғciency. 6. Experiments In this section, we present numerical experiments that il- lustrate our theoretical guarantees on both synthetic and real-world federated learning datasets. Our code is available at https://github.com/albietz/ppsgd. Experiment setup. We consider linear additive models of the form (3) with the squared loss. For classiп¬Ғcation datasets, we use a one-versus-all setup. Unless otherwise mentioned, we run our algorithms for one pass over the data, simulating the online setting considered by our the- ory. We sample a п¬Ғxed number of users (denoted Q) at each iteration, chosen randomly without replacement, and consider minibatches of size m= 10 for each user. Follow- ing standard practice, we compute privacy guarantees Пөat each iteration using the moments accountant (Abadi et al., 2016), with a п¬Ғxed Оҙ= 10вҲ’4, after applying Gaussian noise of variance Пғ2 О¶ = Пғ2C2, where C is a clipping parame- ter and Пғ a noise multiplier. For each run, we consider a п¬Ғxed step-size О·, personalization parameter Оұ, clipping parameter C, and noise multiplier Пғ, chosen from a grid (see Appendix A). In order to assess the best achievable performance, we optimize the learning rate separately at each number of iterations reported in our п¬Ғgures. Synthetic dataset. We begin with a synthetic regression example, where each userвҖҷs data is generated from a ground truth linear model, and multiple coordinates of the parame- ters are shared across users. We consider N = 1000 users and data in d = 100 dimensions. The parameters ОёвҲ— i are generated as follows from a random Оё0 вҲјN(0,102Id): [ОёвҲ— i]1:95 = [Оё0]1:95 [ОёвҲ— i]96:100 = [Оё0]96:100 + Оҙi, Оҙ i вҲјN(0,0.012I), while the samples (xi,yi) for user iare generated by the following process: xi вҲјN(0,ОЈ), ОЈ = diag{О»k}d k=1,О»k = 1/k yi = ОёвҲ—вҠӨ i xi + Оөi, Оө i вҲјN(0,П„2). The black curve also optimizes over the level of personalization, which improves the tradeoff. In Figure 1b, we show the top-1 accuracy on the test set, while running our method for a single pass over the data, sampling Q = 10 users and m = 10 documents per user at each iteration. Finally, we consider a subset of the federated EMNIST digit classiп¬Ғcation dataset2 consisting of N = 1000 users with about 100 training and 10 test samples per user. Here, a single pass does not lead to good performance, suggesting this may be a hard learning prob- lem that beneп¬Ғts from multiple passes (Pillaud-Vivien et al., 2018). We thus run our method for 20 epochs, sampling 10 users and 10 images per user at each iteration, and show the resulting test accuracy in Figure 1c. On this dataset, we see that local learning does generally poorly, perhaps because there is a lot of shared structure in images across users that beneп¬Ғts signiп¬Ғcantly from global learning. Never- theless, there is still a beneп¬Ғt to using small Оұfor achieving good privacy guarantees, and personalization still plays a role in improving the privacyвҖ“accuracy tradeoff (albeit less pronounced than on Stackoverп¬Ӯow, see Figure 2). In Figure 1d, we show similar plots on federated EM- NIST for a 4-layer CNN with a shared global represen- tation ОҰW(x) and an additive model at the output layer, leading to a loss of the form fi((w,W ),Оёi,(x,y)) = в„“(y,(w+ Оёi)вҠӨОҰW(x)). We see that similar conclusions hold in this non-convex 2https://www.tensorflow.org/federated/ api_docs/python/tff/simulation/datasets/ emnist/load_data scenario, namely, small Оұ is helpful for achieving better privacy guarantees, while large Оұleads to better accuracy for poor privacy levels. Here the local-only models (Оұ= 0) perform very poorly, since the global representation has frozen random weights, highlighting that learning a useful representation in this model requires a privacy cost. 7. Discussion and Conclusion In this paper, we studied personalized private federated opti- mization algorithms, providing generalization guarantees in the convex setting that highlight the role of personalization and privacy. We show both theoretically and empirically that adjusting the amount of personalization is always beneп¬Ғcial for improving the accuracyвҖ“privacy tradeoff, by providing a way to attenuate the cost of privacy through local learning at large sample sizes. Promising future directions include extending our analysis to different models of personaliza- tion, e.g., based on neural networks, and to provide adaptive algorithms which may dynamically adjust the level of per- sonalization in an online fashion. B. Proofs of Main Results For proving the privacy guarantees, we make use of the following billboard lemma. Lemma B.1 (Billboard Lemma, Hsu et al., 2014). Suppose a message broadcasting mechanism M: DвҶ’W is (Пө,Оҙ)- differentially private. Consider any set of functions: gi : DiГ—WвҶ’W вҖІ. The composition {gi(О iD,M(D))}is (Пө,Оҙ)-joint differentially private, where О i : DвҶ’D i is the projection of Donto Di. B.1. Proof of Theorem 4.1 (Privacy for PP-SGD) Proof. To prove the JDP guarantee, by the billboard lemma (Lemma B.1), it is sufп¬Ғcient to show that the global model updates satisfy standard DP w.r.t. any changes to a single userвҖҷs data. With the assumption that gradients are uniformly bounded byG, each update of the global models has в„“2 sensitivity bounded by ОұО·/N w.r.t. any change to a single userвҖҷs data. If we take ПғО¶ вүҘcG вҲҡ nlog(1/Оҙ) NПө , for some absolute constant c, then by the Gaussian mechanism, each update is ( ПөвҲҡ nlog(1/Оҙ) ,Оҙ)-differentially private, and by the advanced composition theorem (Dwork et al., 2010b), the algorithm that outputs global models is (Пө,Оҙ)-differentially private after nsteps.Personalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning When considering both local and global models as outputs of the algorithm, combining the above with the billboard lemma yields the desired JDP guarantee. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper introduces a personalized federated optimization framework under user-level (joint) differential privacy (JDP) and shows that personalization improves the privacyвҖ“accuracy tradeoff. It proposes a simple private personalized SGD (PPSGD) algorithm with a personalization parameter Оұ controlling the balance between global and local updates, proves user-level JDP guarantees via the billboard model, and derives excess-risk generalization bounds where the privacy cost is independent of per-user sample size n. It extends analysis to client sampling and heterogeneous mini-batches, quantifies when personalization helps (a threshold on n), and validates findings on synthetic and real federated datasets (StackOverflow, EMNIST).
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Problem: Minimize f(w,Оё1:N)= (1/N)вҲ‘i fi(w,Оёi) with global parameter w and per-user local parameters Оёi; focus on convex, L-smooth losses, including an additive model в„“(y,(w+Оёi)^T x).
- Algorithm 1 (PPSGD): Each round t, each user i performs a local Оёi update using its stochastic gradient; sends clipped gradient wrt w to server. Server aggregates, adds Gaussian noise, and updates w with step size scaled by Оұ (relative global/local step-size). Оұ tunes personalization: Оұ=0 (local-only), ОұвҶ’вҲһ (global-only).
- Privacy: User-level JDP via billboard model; Gaussian mechanism with per-round clipping. Theorem 4.1: If ПғО¶ вүҘ c C вҲҡ(n log(1/Оҙ)) /(N Оө), the sequence of global updates is (Оө,Оҙ)-DP вҶ’ overall (Оө,Оҙ)-JDP.
- Generalization: SGD-style analysis with preconditioner H inducing Оұ-norm; Theorem 4.5 gives E[f( zМ„n)вҲ’f*] вүӨ O(LОұвҖ–z*вҖ–Оұ^2/n) + O(вҖ–z*вҖ–Оұ вҲҡ(Оұ ПғМ„w^2 + ПғМ„Оё^2/N)/вҲҡn) + O(вҖ–z*вҖ–Оұ вҲҡ(Оұ dw G^2 log(1/Оҙ))/(N Оө)). Privacy term independent of n.
- Client sampling (Algorithm 2): Sample each user with prob q, use mini-batch size mi; optimize weighted objective fm (weights вҲқ mi). Theorem 5.1: privacy amplification by subsampling; ПғО¶ вүҘ c C вҲҡ(T log(1/Оҙ))/(M Оө) with M=вҲ‘i mi. Theorem 5.2: analogous excess-risk bound with extra variance term from client sampling and privacy cost scaling with G mmax/M.
- Variant for unweighted average user performance (Algorithm 3): Average gradients over mini-batches; bound uses N and mmin, improving privacy term (вҲқ1/N) at some statistical efficiency cost.
- Additional results: Lemma 4.6 characterizes when decreasing Оұ helps (threshold on n depending on N, Оө, dw, Пғ). Assumptions include convexity, smoothness, bounded gradients; gradient clipping set to avoid effect (C=G).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Common setup: Linear additive models with squared loss; privacy accounting via moments accountant with Оҙ=1e-4; Gaussian noise with multiplier Пғ and gradient clipping C; learning rate О· and Оұ chosen from grids; one-pass or multi-pass training; per-iteration optimization of О· for fair comparisons.
- Synthetic regression: N=1000 users, d=100; Оё*i shares first 95 coordinates across users with small per-user noise on last 5; x ~ N(0, diag(1/k)); label noise variance П„^2; C=10; one pass; Q=N users per iteration; mini-batch m=10; evaluate excess risk in closed form.
- StackOverflow tag prediction: Subset with N=500 users; 300вҖ“500 train and 10вҖ“100 test docs/user; bag-of-words d=5000; 80 most popular tags (one-vs-all); one pass; Q=10 users/iter; m=10; C=0.01; evaluate top-1 test accuracy; Оө tracked via moments accountant.
- Federated EMNIST (digits): N=1000 users; ~100 train and 10 test images/user.
  вҖў Linear model: d=784Г—10; 20 passes; Q=10, m=10; C=10; test accuracy.
  вҖў CNN: 4-layer CNN with shared representation ОҰ_W and additive output layer; same 20-pass protocol; C=0.1; test accuracy.
- Across datasets: Vary Оұ and Пғ to visualize accuracyвҖ“privacy tradeoffs; compare local-only, global-only, and personalized settings.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Theoretical guarantees assume convexity, L-smoothness, and bounded gradients (C=G), which may not hold for deep non-convex models; CNN results lack theoretical backing.
- Additive personalization model analyzed; more complex personalization structures (e.g., factorized or meta-learning) are not covered by assumptions.
- Clipping impact on optimization is largely sidestepped by assuming C=G; in practice G may be large, weakening privacy or necessitating clipping-induced bias.
- Privacy analysis relies on the billboard model and JDP; assumes a trusted server adding noise and no leakage outside the public messages.
- Privacy cost grows with dimension dw and can dominate in high-dimensional settings; dependence on mmax/M in heterogeneous mini-batch regimes can increase privacy cost for skewed data distributions.
- Bounds require existence of a minimizer with finite Оұ-norm; multiple passes and communication round schedules beyond analyzed settings may affect bias terms.
- Empirical evaluation spans a limited set of tasks/datasets and hyperparameter grids; no system-level constraints (communication/latency/energy) studied; no comparison to strong baselines like local-SGD with formal DP guarantees.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Extend theory to non-convex models (deep networks) and richer personalization architectures (e.g., representation learning, meta-learning, factorized models) under user-level DP/JDP.
- Analyze and design adaptive strategies to tune Оұ online per user/round or per-coordinate, possibly using privacy/variance estimates.
- Provide guarantees for local-SGD/federated averaging and multiple local steps under JDP; study communicationвҖ“privacyвҖ“accuracy tradeoffs and partial participation.
- Tighter analysis of gradient clipping (choice of C), moments accountant usage, and privacy amplification with structured sampling; per-user adaptive clipping/noise.
- Address heterogeneous data: bounds that depend on distributional drift, unbalanced m_i, fairness constraints, and personalized privacy budgets.
- Improve privacy dependence on dimension via dimensionality reduction or private representation learning compatible with personalization.
- Empirical validation at scale on-device; incorporate secure aggregation and system heterogeneity; measure end-to-end latency/energy impacts.
- Explore stronger or alternative privacy notions (pure DP, RГ©nyi DP accounting) and robustness to adversarial or colluding users.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_code:
# Extracted code implementing the PPSGD method, clipping, noise addition, and alpha-controlled personalization

# File: main_real.py (core PPSGD for linear models on real datasets)
from opacus.accountants.rdp import RDPAccountant
import numpy as np
import time

def training_priv(ds, step=0.5, stepw=None, steptheta=None,
                  C=10., noise_mult=0.05, delta=1e-4, eval_delta=500):
    w = np.zeros((ds.d, ds.n_classes))
    thetas = np.zeros((ds.N, ds.d, ds.n_classes))

    priv = RDPAccountant()

    if stepw is None:
        stepw = step
    if steptheta is None:
        steptheta = step

    ls = []
    accs = []
    accs_test = []
    test_iters = []
    epss = []

    for i, (ids, X, y) in enumerate(ds.batches()):
        L = len(ids)
        X = X[:,:L,:]  # [B,N,d]
        y = y[:,:L,:]  # [B,N,K]
        y -= 0.1
        pred = np.sum(X[:,:,:,None] * (w[None,None,:,:] + thetas[None,ids,:,:]), axis=2)
        if i % eval_delta == 0:
            print(i, 'eval test acc...', end='', flush=True)
            t = time.time()
            acc_test = ds.eval_test(w, thetas)
            print(acc_test, '({:.2f})'.format(time.time() - t))
            test_iters.append(i)
            accs_test.append(acc_test)

        thetas_grad = np.sum(X[:,:,:,None] * (pred - y)[:,:,None,:], axis=0) / ds.batch_size # [N,d,K]
        if steptheta > 0:
            thetas[ids] -= steptheta * thetas_grad

        thetas_grad /= np.clip(np.linalg.norm(thetas_grad, axis=(1,2)) / C, a_min=1., a_max=None)[:,None,None]
        w_grad = np.mean(thetas_grad, axis=0)
        if stepw > 0:
            w -= stepw * (w_grad + noise_mult * C * np.random.randn(ds.d, ds.n_classes) / L)
            priv.step(noise_multiplier=noise_mult, sample_rate=L/ds.N)

        eps, best_alpha = priv.get_privacy_spent(delta=delta)
        if i % eval_delta == 0:
            print('eps:', eps)
        epss.append(eps)
    return {'ls': ls, 'accs': accs, 'test_iters': test_iters, 'accs_test': accs_test, 'epss': epss}

# Alpha-to-steps mapping used across experiments (from main_real.py and main_synthetic.py)
# - If 0 <= alpha <= 1: stepw = alpha * step; steptheta = step
# - If alpha == -1 (infinity): stepw = step; steptheta = 0
# - If alpha > 1: stepw = step; steptheta = step / alpha


# File: main_synthetic.py (PPSGD for synthetic linear model)
from opacus.accountants.rdp import RDPAccountant
import numpy as np

def training_priv(ds, batch_size=10,
                  step=0.5, stepw=None, steptheta=None,
                  niter=300, C=10., noise_mult=0.05, sample_rate=1., delta=1e-4):
    w = np.zeros(ds.d)
    thetas = np.zeros((ds.N, ds.d))

    priv = RDPAccountant()

    if stepw is None:
        stepw = step
    if steptheta is None:
        steptheta = step

    ls = []
    epss = []
    for i in range(niter):
        X, y = ds.get_batch(batch_size)
        pred = np.sum(X * (w[None,:] + thetas), axis=2)

        thetas_grad = np.sum(X * (pred - y)[:,:,None], axis=0) / batch_size
        if steptheta > 0:
            thetas -= steptheta * thetas_grad

        thetas_grad /= np.clip(np.linalg.norm(thetas_grad, axis=1) / C, a_min=1., a_max=None)[:,None]
        w_grad = np.mean(thetas_grad, axis=0)
        if stepw > 0:
            w -= stepw * (w_grad + noise_mult * C * np.random.randn(ds.d) / ds.N)

            priv.step(noise_multiplier=noise_mult, sample_rate=1.)

        ls.append(ds.excess_risk(w, thetas))
        eps, best_alpha = priv.get_privacy_spent(delta=delta)
        epss.append(eps)
    return ls, epss


# File: main_nn.py (PPSGD-style with CNN feature extractor in JAX; per-user clipping and Gaussian noise)
from opacus.accountants.rdp import RDPAccountant
from jax import grad, jit, random, vmap
from jax.tree_util import tree_flatten, tree_unflatten
import jax.numpy as jnp
import numpy as np
import time
from jax.example_libraries import stax

fdim = 32

init_random_params, predict_features = stax.serial(
    stax.Conv(16, (8, 8), padding='SAME', strides=(2, 2)),
    stax.Relu,
    stax.MaxPool((2, 2), (1, 1)),
    stax.Conv(32, (4, 4), padding='VALID', strides=(2, 2)),
    stax.Relu,
    stax.MaxPool((2, 2), (1, 1)),
    stax.Flatten,
    stax.Dense(fdim),
    stax.Relu,
)

def predict(params, w, theta, x):
    features = predict_features(params, x)
    return jnp.sum(features[:,:,None] * (w[None,:,:] + theta[None,:,:]), axis=1)

def loss(params, w, theta, x, y):
    return jnp.mean((predict(params, w, theta, x) - y) ** 2)

def clipped_grad(params, w, theta, l2_norm_clip, x, y):
    gp, gw, gth = grad(loss, (0, 1, 2))(params, w, theta, x, y)
    grads, tree_def = tree_flatten(gp)
    total_grad_norm = jnp.sqrt(jnp.sum(gw ** 2) + jnp.array([jnp.sum(g.ravel() ** 2) for g in grads]).sum())
    divisor = jnp.maximum(total_grad_norm / l2_norm_clip, 1.)
    normalized_grads = [g / divisor for g in grads]
    return tree_unflatten(tree_def, normalized_grads), gw / divisor, gth

@jit
def private_grad(params, w, thetas, xs, ys, rng, l2_norm_clip, noise_mult, num_users):
    cgp, cgw, gth = vmap(clipped_grad, (None, None, 0, None, 1, 1))(
        params, w, thetas, l2_norm_clip, xs, ys)
    # p
    cgp_flat, grads_treedef = tree_flatten(cgp)
    cgp_tot = [g.mean(0) for g in cgp_flat]
    rngp, rngw = random.split(rng)
    rngs = random.split(rngp, len(cgp_tot))
    cgp_noised = [g + l2_norm_clip * noise_mult * random.normal(r, g.shape) / num_users
                  for r, g in zip(rngs, cgp_tot)]
    # w
    cgw_tot = cgw.mean(0)
    cgw_noised = cgw_tot + l2_norm_clip * noise_mult * random.normal(rngw, cgw_tot.shape) / num_users
    return tree_unflatten(grads_treedef, cgp_noised), cgw_noised, gth


def training_priv(ds, step=0.5, stepw=None, steptheta=None, stepw_prob=None, 
                  C=10., noise_mult=0.05, delta=1e-4, eval_delta=500):
    key = random.PRNGKey(42)
    _, params = init_random_params(key, (-1, 28, 28, 1))
    w = np.random.randn(fdim, ds.n_classes) / np.sqrt(fdim)
    thetas = np.zeros((ds.N, fdim, ds.n_classes))

    priv = RDPAccountant()

    if stepw is None:
        stepw = step
    if steptheta is None:
        steptheta = step

    ls = []
    accs = []
    accs_test = []
    test_iters = []
    epss = []

    @jit
    def update_params(params, params_grad):
        ps, tree_def = tree_flatten(params)
        gs, gtree_def = tree_flatten(params_grad)
        ps_new = [p - stepw * g for (p, g) in zip(ps, gs)]
        return tree_unflatten(tree_def, ps_new)

    def eval_test(params, w, thetas):
        acc = 0
        cnt = 0
        for i, (X, y) in enumerate(ds.test_datasets):
            preds = predict(params, w, thetas[i], X.reshape(-1, 28, 28, 1))
            acc += np.sum(preds.argmax(1) == y)
            cnt += len(y)

        return acc / cnt

    for i, (ids, X, y) in enumerate(ds.batches()):
        L = len(ids)
        X = X[:,:L,:]  # [B,N,d]
        X = X.reshape(X.shape[0], X.shape[1], 28, 28, 1)
        y = y[:,:L,:]  # [B,N,K]
        y -= 0.1
        if i % eval_delta == 0:
            print(i, 'eval test acc...', end='', flush=True)
            t = time.time()
            acc_test = eval_test(params, w, thetas)
            print(acc_test, '({:.2f})'.format(time.time() - t))
            test_iters.append(i)
            accs_test.append(acc_test)


        rng = random.fold_in(key, i)  # get new key for new random numbers
        params_grad, w_grad, thetas_grad = private_grad(params, w, thetas[ids],
                X, y, rng, C, noise_mult, L)
        if steptheta > 0:
            thetas[ids] -= L * steptheta * thetas_grad

        if stepw > 0:
            w -= stepw * w_grad
            params = update_params(params, params_grad)

            priv.step(noise_multiplier=noise_mult, sample_rate=L/ds.N)

        eps, best_alpha = priv.get_privacy_spent(delta=delta)
        if i % eval_delta == 0:
            print('eps:', eps)
        epss.append(eps)
    return {'ls': ls, 'accs': accs, 'test_iters': test_iters, 'accs_test': accs_test, 'epss': epss}

    'noise_mult': [0.02, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 2., 5.],
    'C': [0.01],
    'rand': [False],
}


# File: main_synthetic.py (alpha-grid for synthetic experiments)
grid = {
    'step': [0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.7, 1., 1.2, 1.5, 1.8],
    'alpha': [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1., 3., 10., 30., 100., -1],

llm_extracted_info.experimental_info:
- Objective and model
  - Minimize f(w, {theta_i}) = (1/N) sum_i f_i(w, theta_i) with additive linear predictors of the form X @ (w + theta_i).
  - Loss used in code: squared loss on one-hot labels (with y shifted by -0.1 for numerical stability in real/NN code).
  - Two model families:
    - Linear models on EMNIST/StackOverflow (main_real.py) and synthetic (main_synthetic.py).
    - CNN feature extractor with a personalized linear head (w, theta_i) in JAX (main_nn.py), feature dimension fdim=32.

- PPSGD update per round (as implemented)
  - Local (client i): compute stochastic gradient w.r.t. theta_i using the clientвҖҷs batch; perform local update: theta_i <- theta_i - steptheta * grad_{theta_i}.
  - Clipping: For each selected client i, clip the local gradient used to form the global update by L2 norm C:
    - Linear/real: clip per-client gradient tensor thetas_grad[i] by its Frobenius/L2 norm across parameters: g_i <- g_i / max(||g_i||/C, 1).
    - JAX/CNN: clip combined norm of global-parameter gradients (features params + w) per-user to C.
  - Server aggregation: w_grad = mean_i g_i over the participating clients in the round.
  - Gaussian mechanism: add zero-mean Gaussian noise to the aggregated w gradient. Standard deviation equals noise_mult * C / L in each coordinate, where L is the number of users in the round.
  - Global update: w <- w - stepw * (w_grad + noise).
  - Privacy accounting: per round, call RDPAccountant.step(noise_multiplier=noise_mult, sample_rate=L/N) and track epsilon for a target delta.

- Personalization knob alpha (alpha controls relative step sizes)
  - If 0 <= alpha <= 1: stepw = alpha * step; steptheta = step (more local personalization as alpha decreases).
  - If alpha == -1 (interpreted as alpha = infinity): global-only, stepw = step; steptheta = 0.
  - If alpha > 1: stepw = step; steptheta = step / alpha (more emphasis on global updates as alpha increases).

- Client sampling
  - Each training round samples L = clients_per_round users uniformly at random (without replacement) via ds.batches().
  - Effective sample rate q = L / N, which is fed to the RDP accountant.

- Differential privacy
  - Mechanism: billboard model with per-round clipping at level C and Gaussian noise with multiplier noise_mult, added to the aggregated global gradient.
  - Accounting: Opacus RDPAccountant with sample_rate=L/N per round and target delta (default 1e-4). Reported epsilon is the composed DP guarantee for the sequence of global updates, implying user-level JDP.

- Datasets and batching
  - EMNIST (Digits) via TensorFlow Federated; features are flattened 28x28 (d=784), n_classes=10; batching within each client with batch_size; each round draws up to L clients and one batch per client.
  - StackOverflow tag prediction: preprocessed pickles of federated per-client datasets; multi-label/multi-class matrices used as provided (n_classes per tags vocab);
  - Synthetic: Gaussian features with per-user personalization controlled by local_weight; exact generation in Dataset.get_batch.

- Key hyperparameters and grids (search ranges)
  - main_real.py (linear EMNIST):
    - step: [1e-4, 2e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3, 2e-3, 5e-3, 1e-2]
    - alpha: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, -1]
    - noise_mult: [2, 5, 20, 50, 70]
    - C: [1, 10, 100]
  - main_real.py (linear StackOverflow):
    - step: [2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250]
    - alpha: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, -1]
    - noise_mult: [0.02, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 2, 5]
    - C: [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10]
  - main_nn.py (CNN EMNIST):
    - step: [0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5]
    - alpha: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, -1]
    - noise_mult: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100]
    - C: [0.1, 1, 10]
  - main_nn.py (CNN StackOverflow):
    - step: [2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250]
    - alpha: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, -1]
    - noise_mult: [0.02, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 2, 5]
    - C: [0.01]
  - main_synthetic.py:
    - step: [0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.7, 1, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8]
    - alpha: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, -1]
    - noise_mult: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, 300, 1000]
    - C: [0.1, 1, 10, 100]

- Default and runtime settings
  - Number of users N: configurable via CLI; examples: EMNIST default 3383; StackOverflow examples use N up to 500.
  - Clients per round L (clients_per_round): configurable via CLI; default 10 or 20 depending on script.
  - Batch size per client b: default 10.
  - Epochs per experiment: num_epochs default 1 (cycling over client batches).
  - Privacy delta: default 1e-4.
  - Evaluation cadence eval_delta: default 500 rounds; report test accuracy and epsilon at those intervals.
  - Random seeds: numpy RandomState seeded (42) in dataset classes; JAX PRNGKey(42) for NN; weights initialized as zeros (linear) or random normal scaled for w in CNN.

- Noise and clipping specifics
  - Clipping threshold C applied per-client per-round to the gradient contribution used for the global update.
  - Gaussian noise added to global update has std = noise_mult * C / L per coordinate (linear); analogous scaling in JAX with l2_norm_clip and division by num_users.

- Mapping to the described method
  - Matches Algorithm 1 (PPSGD): local theta updates, clipped/aggregated global gradient with Gaussian noise, alpha-tuned personalization via step size ratio.
  - User-level DP accounting via RDP with subsampling rate q=L/N reflects billboard model user-level JDP.

research_study_list.title: Personalization Improves Privacy-Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning
research_study_list.abstract:
Large-scale machine learning systems often involve data distributed across a
collection of users. Federated learning algorithms leverage this structure by
communicating model updates to a central server, rather than entire datasets.
In this paper, we study stochastic optimization algorithms for a personalized
federated learning setting involving local and global models subject to
user-level (joint) differential privacy. While learning a private global model
induces a cost of privacy, local learning is perfectly private. We provide
generalization guarantees showing that coordinating local learning with private
centralized learning yields a generically useful and improved tradeoff between
accuracy and privacy. We illustrate our theoretical results with experiments on
synthetic and real-world datasets.
research_study_list.full_text: Personalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning Alberto Bietti 1 Chen-Yu Wei2 Miroslav DudВҙДұk3 John Langford 3 Zhiwei Steven Wu4 Abstract Large-scale machine learning systems often in- volve data distributed across a collection of users. Federated learning algorithms leverage this struc- ture by communicating model updates to a central server, rather than entire datasets. In this paper, we study stochastic optimization algorithms for a personalized federated learning setting involv- ing local and global models subject to user-level (joint) differential privacy. While learning a pri- vate global model induces a cost of privacy, local learning is perfectly private. We provide gener- alization guarantees showing that coordinating local learning with private centralized learning yields a generically useful and improved tradeoff between accuracy and privacy. We illustrate our theoretical results with experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets. 1. Introduction Many modern applications of machine learning involve data from a large set of users. In such settings, both privacy considerations and bandwidth limits may require keeping each userвҖҷs data on their device, instead communicating with a centralized server via shared model updates, a scenario commonly known as federated learning (Kairouz et al., 2021). When the data distribution varies across users, it is often beneп¬Ғcial to consider personalized models where parts of the model ( e.g., user-speciп¬Ғc embeddings, or additive offsets) are local to each user, leading to updates that may be performed locally. Such personalized federated learning has been deployed at scale in a wide range of ap- plications, including keyboard next-word prediction (Wang et al., 2019), automated speech recognition, and news 1Center for Data Science, New York University2University of Southern California 3Microsoft Research, New York 4Carnegie Mellon University. Correspondence to: Alberto Bietti <al- berto.bietti@nyu.edu>. Proceedings of the 39 th International Conference on Machine Learning, Baltimore, Maryland, USA, PMLR 162, 2022. Copy- right 2022 by the author(s). personalization (Paulik et al., 2021). In this paper, we focus on personalized federated learning algorithms based on stochastic optimization, which are the most widely used in this context (McMahan et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021). Concretely, we consider stochastic opti- mization problems of the following form: min w,Оё1:N { f(w,Оё1:N) := 1 N NвҲ‘ i=1 EОҫвҲјPi[fi(w,Оёi,Оҫ)] } , (1) where N is the number of users, w a global parameter, Оё1:N = (Оё1,...,Оё N) a set of local parameters, and Pi de- notes the sample distribution for user i. Similar objectives have been considered in other works (Agarwal et al., 2020; Hanzely et al., 2021). The personalization problem raises the question of how much learning should happen at the local vs global level: if the local models are expressive enough, local learning alone should sufп¬Ғce to learn good models with enough samples per user, yet if the data has some shared structure across users, a global model may help us learn more efп¬Ғciently. Although federated learning algorithms do not store user data in the central server, private user information may still be leaked in the resulting models, unless appropriate mech- anisms are applied to guarantee privacy. In this work, we consider the notion of user-level (joint) differential privacy (DP) (Dwork et al., 2006; 2010a; Kearns et al., 2014), which ensures an adversary cannot reliably detect the presence or absence of all the data associated with a single user based on the output information. Such a notion has been used successfully in federated learning problems, with practical optimization algorithms that lead to useful privacy guaran- tees (McMahan et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2021). Unfortunately, assuring such privacy may come at a cost to accuracy (see, for example, the lower bounds of Bassily et al., 2014). The key question we address is: Can we leverage personalization to improve privacyвҖ“ accuracy tradeoffs in federated learning? The insight motivating our work is that in personalized fed- erated learning, it is only the global portion of the optimiza- tion that needs to experience this drop in accuracy. A userвҖҷs arXiv:2202.05318v2  [stat.ML]  15 Jul 2022Personalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning local model can compensate for the privacy-guaranteeing accuracy limitations of the global model. We formalize this by considering algorithms with a personalization parame- ter Оұthat may vary the level of personalization from local learning (Оұ= 0) to global learning (Оұ= вҲһ). We then show generalization bounds on the objective 1 of the form f(wn,Оё1:N,n)вҲ’fвҲ—вүӨCstat(Оұ,n,N )+ Cpriv(Оұ,Пө,N ), (2) where fвҲ—= minw,Оё1:N f(w,Оё1:N) is the optimal risk, nis the number of observed samples per user and Пөis the DP privacy parameter. Here, we expect the second term to vanish when ОұвҶ’0, as local learning does not suffer from privacy. Crucially, this privacy cost does not depend on the number of samples per user n, while the п¬Ғrst term, which captures statistical efп¬Ғciency, generally decreases with n for any Оұ. This emphasizes how adjusting the level of personalization through Оұcan help improve generalization by adjusting the trade-off between these two terms. Concretely, we provide precise guarantees of this form for simple federated private stochastic gradient algorithms, where the number of iterations corresponds to the number of samples per user n, and the personalization parameter Оұ is given by the relative step-size between global and local updates. In particular, we show thatОұaffects the complexity of learning by changing the geometry of the optimization: in problems that beneп¬Ғt from global models, small Оұmakes learning more difп¬Ғcult but reduces the cost of privacy. We complement our theoretical results with experiments on synthetic and real-world federated learning datasets, which illustrate how varying the step-size ratio leads to improved trade-offs between accuracy and privacy. 2. Related Work Model personalizaton. There are a variety of approaches for personalization in federated learning. In local п¬Ғne- tuning, a global model is learnt by federated learning and then used as a warm start for on-device learning from the cache of local data (Wang et al., 2019; Paulik et al., 2021). This approach can be augmented with federated learning of hyperparameters (Wang et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2019; Khodak et al., 2019) to obtain federated learning variants of meta-learning approaches like MAML (Finn et al., 2017) and Reptile (Nichol et al., 2018). Another approach is to view personalization as a multi-task learning problem, and learn task-speciп¬Ғc models with a regularization that forces parameters for similar tasks to be close, e.g., in a Euclidean norm (Vanhaesebrouck et al., 2017; Smith et al., 2017; Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Mansour et al., 2020; Dinh et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021; Marfoq et al., 2021; Singhal et al., 2021). Task simi- larity is typically expressed as a weighted undirected graph, a matrix, or a clustering. A special case of the multi-task ap- proach is to learn a global model in addition to local models, which are regularized to be close to the global model (Man- sour et al., 2020; Deng et al., 2020; Hanzely & Richt Вҙarik, 2020; Hanzely et al., 2021; Marfoq et al., 2021). This is closest to the approach here, which also separates global and local parameters. However, the approach here is more general, because we allow a broader range of modeling rela- tionships between the global and local parameters, similar to federated residual learning (Agarwal et al., 2020). The sta- tistical aspects of such personalization models were studied by Mansour et al. (2020); Agarwal et al. (2020), who in par- ticular provide generalization guarantees for additive person- alization models similar to ours. However, compared to the present paper, these works do not provide privacy guarantees nor study the effect of varying the level of personalization. Privacy. The results here advance a recent line of work that provides formal user-level privacy guarantees for model personalizaton in federated learning. Similar to the prior work of Jain et al. (2021) and Hu et al. (2021), we adopt the privacy formulation of joint differential privacy (JDP, Kearns et al., 2014), a variation of DP that is more suitable for the problem of model personalization than standard DP. Jain et al. (2021) provides private algorithms that п¬Ғrst learn a shared linear representation for all users, and allow each user to learn their local linear regression model over the lower-dimensional representation. This leads to a factorized model of personalization, which is different than ours and not handled by our theoretical assumptions due to non-convexity. Hu et al. (2021) provides a private personalizaton algorithm through the mean-regularized multi-task learning objective without establishing its statistical rates. In contrast, we consider more general personalization schemes and provide statistical guarantees. The results here are also related to other work in DP with a similar motivation to model personalization. Li et al. (2020) studies meta-learning under DP. Their framework does not cover model personalization with a separate model for each user. Noble et al. (2021) studies federated learning with DP with heterogeneous data across nodes, but they do not support personalization. Bellet et al. (2018) studies fully decentralized DP algorithms for collaborative learning over a network instead of federated learning. Finally, the notion of user-level privacy has also been adopted in prior works (McMahan et al., 2018; Levy et al., 2021), but these do not consider model personalization. 3. Preliminaries In this section, we introduce the problem of personalized federated optimization, as well as the notion of user-level privacy that we consider.Personalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning 3.1. Problem setting We consider stochastic optimization problems of the form min w,Оё1:N { f(w,Оё1:N) := 1 N NвҲ‘ i=1 fi(w,Оёi) } , where N is the number of users, w вҲҲRdw a global pa- rameter, Оё1:N = ( Оё1,...,Оё N) вҲҲ (RdОё)N a set of local parameters, and fi(w,Оёi) := EОҫвҲјPi[fi(w,Оёi,Оҫ)] is the ex- pected risk of user i, with random samples Оҫdrawn from an unknown user-speciп¬Ғc distribution Pi. While our algorithms may be run on arbitrary differen- tiable models, our analysis focuses on the convex setting, where fi(w,Оёi,Оҫi) is jointly convex in (w,Оёi) for all Оҫi. Additive model. An important special case is the additive model for supervised learning, where dw = dОё = d, and fi(w,Оёi,(x,y)) = в„“(y,(w+ Оёi)вҠӨx), (3) where в„“is a loss function andОҫ= (x,y) is a training sample. As a running example, consider a movie recommendation app, which seeks to predict how each user iwill rate any given movie. In this case, x corresponds to the features describing a movie (e.g., its genre, popularity, length, actors, etc.), and yis the userвҖҷs rating of that movie. Following the federated protocol, the data about user activity stays on the device and only model parameters can be communicated to the server. In this case, only the information about the global parameter wis communicated. The additive model makes the optimization problem (1) underdetermined, with many possible equivalent solutions obtained by adding any vector v вҲҲRd to wand then sub- tracting v from each Оёi, effectively вҖңshiftingвҖқ the predic- tive ability between global and local parameters. This is what allows the optimization algorithm to achieve different tradeoffs between statistical generalization (accuracy) and sharing of information across users (privacy). To develop the intuition about this tradeoff, п¬Ғrst consider the homogeneous scenario, where the optimal parame- ters ОёвҲ— i вҲҲarg minОёE(x,y)вҲјPi[в„“(y,ОёвҠӨx)] for each user are equal (ОёвҲ— 1 = В·В·В· = ОёвҲ— N). There are two extreme approaches: (i) local learning, where only Оёi are trained individually for each user, leading to poor sample complexity but per- fect privacy (ii) global learning, where only wis trained, and we beneп¬Ғt from using samples from all users, but need communication with a centralized server and lose privacy. In the more realistic heterogeneous scenario, when the ОёвҲ— i are different but have some shared components, e.g., only some coordinates differ across users, joint learning of w and Оё1:N can achieve greater accuracy (at the same number of samples) than both local and global learning, by leverag- ing more samples to estimate the shared components, while beneп¬Ғting from user-speciп¬Ғc data to personalize. Note that in this case, it is possible to further improve privacy, by al- lowing some shared components to be п¬Ғtted entirely locally. Quantifying this improvement is the focus of our work. In the movie recommendation example, if only global learn- ing is performed, the prediction wвҠӨxcan use overall popu- larity statistics, but the resulting prediction is the same for all users, without any personalization. On the other hand, if only local learning is performed, although the system can fully personalize and provide full privacy, the quality of rec- ommendation is limited by the number of movies the user watched before. With joint learning, the system can cap- ture both global trends through wand adapt to each userвҖҷs preference through Оёi. 3.2. User-level (joint) differential privacy We aim to provide user-level privacy (Dwork et al., 2010a), which ensures that an adverary cannot detect the presence or absence of all of the data associated with a single user when given the output of the algorithm. To achieve this goal, we design algorithms using the billboard model (Hsu et al., 2014) ofdifferential privacy(DP) (Dwork et al., 2006). Let us п¬Ғrst revisit the deп¬Ғnition of DP, which informally requires that changing any single userвҖҷs data cannot change the algorithmвҖҷs output by much. Deп¬Ғnition 3.1 (User-level DP; Dwork et al., 2006; 2010a). A randomized mechanism M is (Пө,Оҙ)-differential privacy (DP) if for all pairs of data sets D,DвҖІthat differ by a single userвҖҷs data and all eventsEin the output range, Pr[M(D) вҲҲE] вүӨeПөPr[M(DвҖІ) вҲҲE] + Оҙ. Billboard model. In the billboard model, a server com- putes aggregate information subject to the constraint of DP and shares the information as public messages with all N users. Then, based on the public messages and their own private data, each user computes their own personalized model. The billboard model is particularly compatible with algorithms in the federated setting, where the DP messages are typically noisy summary statistics about the usersвҖҷ lo- cal models (Jain et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2021). Hsu et al. (2014) show that algorithms under the billboard model pro- vide an extremely strong privacy guarantee, known as joint differential privacy (JDP, Kearns et al., 2014). Joint differential privacy (JDP). Let Di denote the col- lection of samples associated with each user i. Two datasets Dand DвҖІare called i-neighbors if they only differ by user iвҖҷs private data. For any mechanismM, we denote MвҲ’i(D) as the output information to all other users except user i.Personalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning Algorithm 1 Personalized-Private-SGD (PPSGD) 1: Input: О·: step-size, Оұ: global/local ratio, ПғО¶: privacy noise level, C: clipping parameter. 2: Initialize w0 = Оё0 = 0. 3: for t= 1 to ndo 4: for all clients iin parallel do 5: Sample data Оҫi,t вҲјPi 6: Compute gt Оё,i = вҲҮОёfi(wtвҲ’1,Оёi,tвҲ’1,Оҫi,t) gt w,i = вҲҮwfi(wtвҲ’1,Оёi,tвҲ’1,Оҫi,t) 7: Update Оёi,t = Оёi,tвҲ’1 вҲ’О· Ngt Оё,i 8: Clip gradient: Лңgt w,i = gt w,i/max(1, вҲҘgt w,iвҲҘ C ) 9: Send Лңgt w,i to the server 10: end for 11: Sample О¶t вҲјN(0,Пғ2 О¶Idw) 12: Update wt = wtвҲ’1 вҲ’ОұО·( 1 N вҲ‘N i=1 Лңgt w,i + О¶t) 13: end for Deп¬Ғnition 3.2 (Joint-Differential Privacy, JDP). An algo- rithm M is (Пө,Оҙ)-jointly differentially private, written as (Пө,Оҙ)-JDP, if for all i, all i-neighboring datasets D,DвҖІ, and all events Ein the output space to all other users except i, Pr[MвҲ’i(D) вҲҲE] вүӨeПөPr[MвҲ’i(DвҖІ) вҲҲE] + Оҙ. In the setting of model personalization, JDP implies that even if all of users except i collude, potentially pooling their private data and local models together, user iвҖҷs private data are still protected, so long as idoes not reveal their own model. 4. Main Algorithm and Analysis Our main algorithm, shown in Algorithm 1, is a personalized version of distributed SGD (Dekel et al., 2012), with a per- sonalization parameter Оұthat controls the relative step-size between global and local updates. At each round t, each user isamples a fresh datapoint Оҫi,t (this could also be a mini-batch), updates its local model Оёi, and sends the gradient with respect towto the central server, which aggregates gradients of all users before updating the global model w. The total number of rounds nthus corre- sponds to the number of samples per user. In order to guar- antee user-level privacy, we clip each userвҖҷs gradientsgt w,i before aggregation, and add Gaussian noise, following com- mon practice (Abadi et al., 2016; McMahan et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2020). The choice of Оұaffects the degree by which the algorithm favors local learning over global learning, with Оұ= 0 forcing local-only updates. Privacy analysis. We п¬Ғrst establish the formal user-level JDP guarantee of Algorithm 1. Theorem 4.1 (Privacy). Suppose we set the noise parameter ПғО¶ вүҘcC вҲҡ nlog(1/Оҙ) NПө , (4) for an absolute constant c, then Algorithm 1 satisп¬Ғes (Пө,Оҙ)- JDP in the billboard model. We defer the full proof to Appendix B.1. At a high level, the proof п¬Ғrst shows that the aggregate information released by the server (the sequence of global model updates) satisп¬Ғes (Пө,Оҙ)-DP. Since the sequence of global models are sufп¬Ғcient statistics for each user to identify their personalized model Оёi, the JDP guarantee follows from the billboard lemma (see Lemma B.1). Generalization analysis. Our generalization analysis re- lies on the following assumptions. We begin with an assump- tion about minimizers of f, which relies on the following norm for a vector z= (w,Оё1:N) that captures the geometry induced by the personalization parameter Оұ: вҲҘzвҲҘ2 Оұ := 1 ОұвҲҘwвҲҘ2 + вҲҘОё1:NвҲҘ2. (5) Assumption 4.2 (Minimizers). f admits a minimizer zвҲ— with п¬Ғnite norm вҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұ. In the case of local learning (Оұ= 0), this implies zвҲ—must have no global component. For joint learning, different min- imizers might exist, and our bounds scale with the minimal norm вҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұ among all such minimizers. Assumption 4.3 (Convexity and smoothness). For all iand Pi-almost every Оҫ, the function (w,Оёi) вҶҰвҶ’fi(w,Оёi,Оҫ) is jointly convex and L-smooth (its gradients are L-Lipschitz). Note that this implies that f(z) is jointly convex in z = (w,Оё1:N). If we consider the example fi(w,Оёi,(x,y)) = 1 2 (yвҲ’(w+ Оёi)вҠӨx)2, and assume вҲҘxвҲҘвүӨ Ralmost surely, it is easy to verify that the assumption holds with L= 2R2. We will also make the following boundedness assumption on gradients, which is commonly made in the context of private stochastic optimization (e.g., Bassily et al., 2014; Feldman et al., 2020), and simpliп¬Ғes our analysis by avoiding the need to study the effect of clipping on optimization. Assumption 4.4 (Bounded gradients). For all i, w, Оёi, and Pi-almost every Оҫ, we have вҲҘвҲҮwfi(w,Оёi,Оҫ)вҲҘвүӨ G. This assumptions avoid the need to clip gradients whenC вүҘ G, and our analysis hereafter assumes C = G. We note that Gmay be large in some cases, growing with the norm of optimal parameters, thus smaller values of Cmay often be beneп¬Ғcial in practice for better privacy guarantees. Gradient variances. We consider the following variance quantities, which we assume to be п¬Ғnite, and which arePersonalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning obtained by considering gradients at a given minimizer zвҲ—: Пғ2 w,i = EОҫвҲҘвҲҮwfi(wвҲ—,ОёвҲ— i,Оҫ) вҲ’вҲҮwfi(wвҲ—,ОёвҲ— i)вҲҘ2 Пғ2 Оё,i = EОҫвҲҘвҲҮОёfi(wвҲ—,ОёвҲ— i,Оҫ)вҲҘ2 ВҜПғ2 w = 1 N вҲ‘ i Пғ2 w,i, ВҜПғ2 Оё = 1 N вҲ‘ i Пғ2 Оё,i. As an example, note that in a simple additive model(3) with squared loss and additive label noise of variance П„2, we have Пғ2 w,i = Пғ2 Оё,i = П„2 Tr(EPi[xxвҠӨ]), recovering standard statistical quantities. If the algorithm relies on mini-batches of size m instead of single datapoints, we may then re- place Пғ2 w,i and Пғ2 Оё,i by Пғ2 w,i/mand Пғ2 Оё,i/m, respectively, by averaging gradients over the mini-batches. We now provide our main result on the convergence rate of Algorithm 1, which also yields a generalization bound on the excess risk. The proof is in Appendix B.2. Theorem 4.5 (Generalization). Under Assumptions 4.2, 4.3, and 4.4, let zвҲ—= (wвҲ—,Оё1:N вҲ—) be any minimizer of f, LОұ := Lmax(Оұ, 1 N), and Пғ2 tot,Оұ := ОұВҜПғ2 w + ВҜПғ2 Оё N + ОұdwПғ2 О¶. (6) With О· = min { 1 4LОұ , вҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұвҲҡnПғtot,Оұ }and C = G, Algorithm 1 satisп¬Ғes E[f(ВҜzn) вҲ’f(zвҲ—)] вүӨ4LОұвҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘ2 Оұ n + 3Пғtot,ОұвҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұвҲҡn , (7) with ВҜzn = 1 n вҲ‘nвҲ’1 t=0 zt. In particular, with ПғО¶ as in (4), hid- ing absolute constants, we have the following generalization bound: E[f(ВҜzn) вҲ’f(zвҲ—)] вүІ (8) LОұвҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘ2 Оұ n + вҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұ вҲҡ ОұВҜПғ2w + ВҜПғ2 Оё Nn + вҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұ вҲҡ ОұdwG2 log(1 Оҙ) N2Пө2 (9) The generalization bound takes the form (2), with a cost of privacy that does not depend on the number of samples per user n. As is common in the analysis of SGD, our bound displays a bias term that decays as 1/n, and a vari- ance term decaying as 1/вҲҡn, controlled by the gradient variance Пғ2 tot,Оұ. Ignoring the privacy noise, given that we use N samples at each round, this variance scales as 1/N, leading to an overall asymptotic rate of 1/ вҲҡ Nn, where Nn is the total sample size. Using minibatches of size mwould further improve this to 1/ вҲҡ Nnm, where the total number of samples is now Nnm. Local vs global learning. Note that when multiple min- imizers zвҲ— exist, one may choose the one with minimal norm вҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұ. If we consider the additive model (3), we may always consider a minimizer of the form zвҲ— = (0,ОёвҲ— 1:N). Then вҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұ is bounded even for Оұ = 0 (local learning), which yields an excess risk of order LвҲ‘ iвҲҘОёвҲ— iвҲҘ2 Nn + вҲҡ ВҜПғ2 Оё вҲ‘ iвҲҘОёвҲ— iвҲҘ2 Nn . In particular, there is no cost for privacy. If we further assume ОёвҲ— i = vвҲ—for all i, this becomes LвҲҘvвҲ—вҲҘ2 n + вҲҘvвҲ—вҲҘ вҲҡ ВҜПғ2 Оё n . We see slow convergence that does not improve with N, which is expected given that no information is shared across users. In this setting, we may instead consider a different minimizer zвҲ—= (vвҲ—,0), for which taking the limit ОұвҶ’вҲһ (global learning) yields the excess risk of order LвҲҘvвҲ—вҲҘ2 n + вҲҘvвҲ—вҲҘ вҲҡ ВҜПғ2w Nn + вҲҘvвҲ—вҲҘ вҲҡ dwG2 log(1 Оҙ) N2Пө2 . The variance term now decays faster for large N, but has an additional privacy term, which decreases quickly withNbut does not improve with large nand may be quite large, par- ticularly in high dimensions, consistent with lower bounds for differentially private optimization (Bassily et al., 2014). Characterizing beneп¬Ғts of personalization. Depending on the number of samples per user n, it may be helpful to choose varying levels of personalization Оұ to adjust this tradeoff, from large Оұ for small nto small Оұ for large n when the privacy cost dominates the bound. The next lemma quantiп¬Ғes this tradeoff for the additive model with homoge- neous users (i.e., ОёвҲ— 1 = В·В·В· = ОёвҲ— N): Lemma 4.6 (Threshold on nfor personalization beneп¬Ғts). Assume (v,0) is a minimizer of f, with f an additive model of the form (3). For any Оұ, the minimizer with minimal Оұ-norm is given by (w,Оё1:N) with w = ОұN ОұN+1 vand Оёi = 1 ОұN+1 v. Assuming ВҜПғw = ВҜПғОё = Пғ, the variance term in the excess risk (9) with nsamples per user is monotonic in Оұ, taking the form: вҲҘvвҲҘ вҲҡ N ОұN + 1 ((Оұ+ 1)Пғ2 Nn + ОұdwG2 log(1/Оҙ) N2Пө2 ) . This is non-decreasing with Оұif and only if nвүі N(N вҲ’1)Пғ2Пө2 dwG2 log(1/Оҙ) . (10) Thus, if we ignore the bias term in (9), this suggests that when (10) holds, using local learning (smaller Оұ) shouldPersonalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning help improve generalization, while if the reverse inequality holds, global learning should be preferred. Eq.(10) suggests that this threshold on the user sample size nscales quadrati- cally with the number of users N, linearly with the privacy level Пө, and inversely with the dimension dw. When the optimal user parameters are different, this transition would likely happen for smaller n, as we expect local models to be useful even at small sample sizes regardless of privacy. Improving the bias term. We remark that the bias term decreases as 1/nin both local and global learning scenarios described above, and does not improve with the number of users. As in standard SGD ( e.g., Dekel et al., 2012), this term decreases with the number of rounds, and may thus decrease more quickly with the number of samples if more communication rounds are performed for the same total number of samples. In Section 5, we show that sampling users at each round can help improve this term. Comparison to Local SGD. A common approach for fed- erated optimization is the local SGD (or federated averaging) algorithm (McMahan et al., 2017), which performs multiple local steps before communicating with the server. This is in contrast to our method, which more closely resembles mini-batch SGD. We note that despite its practical success, the known theoretical guarantees of local SGD typically do not improve on mini-batch SGD except for speciп¬Ғc set- tings (Woodworth et al., 2020). Our study therefore focuses on understanding personalization in the SGD setting, but we note that extending our analysis to local SGD is an inter- esting direction for future work. 5. Heterogeneous Sample Sizes, User Sampling In this section, we study a variant of Algorithm 1 where we sample some of the users uniformly at each round, with possibly different minibatch sizes for each user. This reп¬Ӯects the fact that users may have different amounts of data, and that not all clients may be available at each round. Let qbe the probability of sampling any given user at each round, mi вүҘ1 be the mini-batch size for user i, and de- п¬Ғne M = вҲ‘ imi and mmax = maximi. Compared to Theorem 4.1, it also leverages a standard argument of privacy ampliп¬Ғcation by subsampling. Theorem 5.1 (Privacy with client sampling). There exist absolute constants c1, c2 such that for any Пө<c 1q2T, if we take ПғО¶ вүҘc2 C вҲҡ Tlog(1/Оҙ) MПө , (12) then Algorithm 2 satisп¬Ғes (Пө,Оҙ)-JDP . Of course, this comes at the cost of more frequent communication rounds, since each round only uses the data of a single user. Optimizing average user performance. This highlights that this improvement in privacy comes at a cost in statistical efп¬Ғciency. 6. Experiments In this section, we present numerical experiments that il- lustrate our theoretical guarantees on both synthetic and real-world federated learning datasets. Our code is available at https://github.com/albietz/ppsgd. Experiment setup. We consider linear additive models of the form (3) with the squared loss. For classiп¬Ғcation datasets, we use a one-versus-all setup. Unless otherwise mentioned, we run our algorithms for one pass over the data, simulating the online setting considered by our the- ory. We sample a п¬Ғxed number of users (denoted Q) at each iteration, chosen randomly without replacement, and consider minibatches of size m= 10 for each user. Follow- ing standard practice, we compute privacy guarantees Пөat each iteration using the moments accountant (Abadi et al., 2016), with a п¬Ғxed Оҙ= 10вҲ’4, after applying Gaussian noise of variance Пғ2 О¶ = Пғ2C2, where C is a clipping parame- ter and Пғ a noise multiplier. For each run, we consider a п¬Ғxed step-size О·, personalization parameter Оұ, clipping parameter C, and noise multiplier Пғ, chosen from a grid (see Appendix A). In order to assess the best achievable performance, we optimize the learning rate separately at each number of iterations reported in our п¬Ғgures. Synthetic dataset. We begin with a synthetic regression example, where each userвҖҷs data is generated from a ground truth linear model, and multiple coordinates of the parame- ters are shared across users. We consider N = 1000 users and data in d = 100 dimensions. The parameters ОёвҲ— i are generated as follows from a random Оё0 вҲјN(0,102Id): [ОёвҲ— i]1:95 = [Оё0]1:95 [ОёвҲ— i]96:100 = [Оё0]96:100 + Оҙi, Оҙ i вҲјN(0,0.012I), while the samples (xi,yi) for user iare generated by the following process: xi вҲјN(0,ОЈ), ОЈ = diag{О»k}d k=1,О»k = 1/k yi = ОёвҲ—вҠӨ i xi + Оөi, Оө i вҲјN(0,П„2). With this setup, the excess risk can be computed in closed form, and is given by 1 N вҲ‘ iвҲҘw+ОёiвҲ’ОёвҲ— iвҲҘ2 ОЈ, and is shown in Figure 1a. We observe that global learning (Оұ= вҲһ) enjoys faster convergence at initial iterations in all cases, thanks to better data efп¬Ғciency, but eventually stops improving, even without privacy noise, since minimizing risk requires personalization in this model. Finally, we consider a subset of the federated EMNIST digit classiп¬Ғcation dataset2 consisting of N = 1000 users with about 100 training and 10 test samples per user. Here, a single pass does not lead to good performance, suggesting this may be a hard learning prob- lem that beneп¬Ғts from multiple passes (Pillaud-Vivien et al., 2018). We thus run our method for 20 epochs, sampling 10 users and 10 images per user at each iteration, and show the resulting test accuracy in Figure 1c. On this dataset, we see that local learning does generally poorly, perhaps because there is a lot of shared structure in images across users that beneп¬Ғts signiп¬Ғcantly from global learning. Never- theless, there is still a beneп¬Ғt to using small Оұfor achieving good privacy guarantees, and personalization still plays a role in improving the privacyвҖ“accuracy tradeoff (albeit less pronounced than on Stackoverп¬Ӯow, see Figure 2). In Figure 1d, we show similar plots on federated EM- NIST for a 4-layer CNN with a shared global represen- tation ОҰW(x) and an additive model at the output layer, leading to a loss of the form fi((w,W ),Оёi,(x,y)) = в„“(y,(w+ Оёi)вҠӨОҰW(x)). We see that similar conclusions hold in this non-convex 2https://www.tensorflow.org/federated/ api_docs/python/tff/simulation/datasets/ emnist/load_data scenario, namely, small Оұ is helpful for achieving better privacy guarantees, while large Оұleads to better accuracy for poor privacy levels. Here the local-only models (Оұ= 0) perform very poorly, since the global representation has frozen random weights, highlighting that learning a useful representation in this model requires a privacy cost. 7. Discussion and Conclusion In this paper, we studied personalized private federated opti- mization algorithms, providing generalization guarantees in the convex setting that highlight the role of personalization and privacy. We show both theoretically and empirically that adjusting the amount of personalization is always beneп¬Ғcial for improving the accuracyвҖ“privacy tradeoff, by providing a way to attenuate the cost of privacy through local learning at large sample sizes. Promising future directions include extending our analysis to different models of personaliza- tion, e.g., based on neural networks, and to provide adaptive algorithms which may dynamically adjust the level of per- sonalization in an online fashion.
